_meta.description: SEO-20 per-track quintet data for the 'generalist' track. Feeds six pages at /roadmap/generalist/{skills,tools,projects,certifications,salary,career-path}. Salary bands use US market figures from levels.fyi / Glassdoor / BLS 2025-2026; regional multipliers are aggregated estimates. Renewable yearly — bump last_updated + regenerate velocity snapshot from the jobs pipeline.

name: AI Generalist
tagline: A broad, balanced foundation across the AI stack — math, classic ML, deep learning, LLMs, and production — so you can follow the field without locking into a narrow specialty in year one.
hero_blurb: The generalist track is for people who want working AI fluency before they pick a specialization. You will ship a portfolio of six projects across classical ML, deep learning, and LLM applications, and leave with the background to move into AI Engineer, ML Engineer, or Data Scientist work within a year.

skills.intro: The AI Generalist skill map below is calibrated against 2026 hiring signals — specifically, the skills that appear in ≥30% of junior-to-mid AI postings across the AutomateEdge jobs pipeline over the last 90 days. Proficiency levels follow a four-step ladder: Beginner (can follow tutorials), Intermediate (can build a solution to a new problem), Advanced (can debug production issues), Expert (can design systems from first principles). A generalist clears Intermediate across every category by week 24; Advanced follows specialization.

categories.name: Programming foundations
categories.blurb: Python is non-negotiable for AI work — every major library, every training framework, every inference runtime has a Python surface. Strong Python means fluent async I/O, type hints, virtual environments, and the standard-library tools (subprocess, pathlib, concurrent.futures) you will reach for when wiring models into applications.

items.note: match-case, positional-only args, walrus, structural typing; rule of thumb — you can read and write it without googling daily.
broadcasting, slicing, einsum, random generators, axis semantics.
groupby, merge, pivot, vectorised ops. Avoid iterrows — it's the #1 junior-interview trap.
FastAPI handlers, httpx streaming, asyncio.gather. Critical once you ship AI APIs.
Generics, TypedDict, Protocols. Shrinks debugging time dramatically.
fixtures, parametrize, monkeypatch, pytest-asyncio. At least 60% line coverage on every portfolio project.
branching, rebase, squash, resolving conflicts, PR etiquette. Hiring managers read git history.
grep, awk, ssh, docker build/run. Enough to debug a VPS deploy without panic.

categories.name: Mathematics for AI
categories.blurb: The math you actually use as a generalist is thinner than most curricula imply. Linear algebra and probability do 80% of the work; calculus appears only through the autograd abstraction you mostly trust. Skip real analysis, skip measure theory — come back for them only if you deep-dive into research.

items.note: dot product as similarity, matrix as linear transform, SVD as the 'what's really there' operation.
joint/conditional/marginal, expectation, variance, the 'likelihood × prior' shape of every learning algorithm.
You will rarely differentiate by hand, but you must read a gradient expression and know what it's saying.
p-values, power, t-test vs chi-square. Needed the moment you A/B-test a model.
SGD, Adam, AdamW, learning-rate schedules, momentum. Not just 'which optimizer' — why each exists.
Entropy, cross-entropy, KL divergence. Core to understanding every loss function.

categories.name: Classical machine learning
categories.blurb: Classical ML is where every generalist builds intuition. Before you can evaluate an LLM response or debug a deep-net loss curve, you need to have trained, over-fit, regularised, and cross-validated on tabular data. It remains the right tool for 60% of real business problems.

items.note: pipelines, ColumnTransformer, cross_val_score, GridSearchCV, the fit/transform contract.
regularisation (L1/L2), multicollinearity, interpreting coefficients.
Still the winning algorithm on most Kaggle tabular competitions. Learn feature importance + SHAP.
k-means, DBSCAN, PCA, t-SNE, UMAP. Knowing when to reach for each.
accuracy, precision, recall, F1, ROC-AUC, PR-AUC, RMSE, MAE. Pick the metric that matches the business cost.
categorical encoding, feature crosses, date-time features, target encoding pitfalls.
Stratified splits, time-series splits, leakage prevention, the #1 source of silent bugs.

categories.name: Deep learning
categories.blurb: PyTorch is the field's lingua franca in 2026. JAX has a loyal niche but if you learn one, learn PyTorch — every paper ships a PyTorch reference implementation. Focus on the primitives (tensors, autograd, nn.Module) before reaching for higher-level wrappers.

items.note: device placement, gradient accumulation, custom backward functions.
forward, loss, backward, optimizer step — write it manually at least once before using Lightning.
conv, pool, batchnorm. Fine-tune a pretrained resnet on your own dataset.
Why attention replaced recurrence. Multi-head attention, positional encoding, causal masks.
Freeze / unfreeze, LoRA basics, data augmentation strategy.
Loss curves, gradient norms, weight histograms. Catching divergence before it wastes a GPU-week.

categories.name: LLMs + generative AI
categories.blurb: LLMs are the fastest-moving surface in the field. A generalist does not need to pre-train one, but must be fluent at wiring hosted models into applications, building retrieval systems, evaluating outputs, and knowing when a smaller classical model would win.

items.note: system/user/assistant turns, few-shot, chain-of-thought, structured output, Pydantic-validated JSON.
chunking, embeddings, vector DBs (pgvector / Pinecone / Weaviate), hybrid search, re-ranking.
automatic evals (exact match, rouge, BLEU, LLM-as-judge), rubric-based, pairwise preference.
Function calling, Anthropic/OpenAI tool APIs, the ReAct loop, when agents are the wrong answer.
Context size vs cost, prompt caching, streaming, model routing, when to distill to a smaller model.
PII redaction, jailbreak patterns, refusal classifiers, content moderation APIs.

categories.name: Production + deployment
categories.blurb: A generalist who cannot deploy is 60% of a hire. Your portfolio projects must live on the public internet — a Docker Compose VPS is a complete, cost-controlled answer. Employers read 'I shipped it' as a hard multiplier on every other skill.

items.note: routers, dependencies, Pydantic models, background tasks, WebSockets for streaming.
multi-stage builds, volume persistence, health checks, reverse-proxy routing.
Postgres or SQLite. Indexes, transactions, migrations (alembic), explain plans.
Structured logging, request IDs, prompt/response logs with PII redaction, uptime monitoring.
GitHub Actions lint + test on PR. A failing test blocks merge — non-negotiable.

learning_sequence: Weeks 1-4 — Python + NumPy/pandas + Git. Ship a data-cleaning notebook with a public dataset.
Weeks 5-8 — Linear algebra + probability via 3Blue1Brown + Khan Academy. Re-derive linear regression from scratch.
Weeks 9-12 — scikit-learn end-to-end. Train, evaluate, and deploy a tree model behind FastAPI.
Weeks 13-16 — PyTorch fundamentals + one CNN and one transformer project on a real dataset.
Weeks 17-20 — LLM APIs + RAG system. Ship a retrieval-backed Q&A app with evals.
Weeks 21-24 — Integration project combining classic ML + LLM, deployed to a VPS, documented in a public README.
skills.time_to_proficient: Full-time learner: 5-6 months to Intermediate across all categories. Part-time (8-10 h/week): 9-12 months. What matters most for hiring is not speed but shipped artifacts — a well-documented GitHub with three deployed projects beats a completion certificate from a six-month bootcamp.

faqs.q: Do I need to memorise calculus before starting?
faqs.a: No. You need gradient descent intuition — 'the model nudges its weights in the direction that reduces loss.' Formal derivatives appear rarely in a generalist's daily work. Revisit calculus if you specialise in research or optimization.

faqs.q: Should I learn TensorFlow or PyTorch?
faqs.a: PyTorch. ~95% of new research code, almost every Hugging Face model, and most production serving stacks are PyTorch-first in 2026. TensorFlow remains entrenched in large-enterprise MLOps stacks — learn it later if you hit a role that requires it.

faqs.q: How much math is 'enough'?
faqs.a: Linear algebra through SVD, probability through Bayes, calculus through the chain rule, optimization through Adam. That set covers 95% of generalist work. Beyond that, math is field-specific — NLP needs information theory, vision needs Fourier, RL needs dynamic programming.

faqs.q: Is Kaggle a good use of time for a generalist?
faqs.a: For weeks 1-12, yes — Kaggle tabular competitions teach feature engineering and evaluation discipline better than most courses. After that, diminishing returns. Shift to deploying personal projects that employers can actually click.

faqs.q: What if I skip classical ML and start with LLMs?
faqs.a: You will plateau within four months. Classical ML teaches the concepts that make LLM work legible — overfitting, regularisation, evaluation, baselines. LLM-first learners routinely fail debugging questions about why a model is wrong.

faqs.q: How do I know I have actually cleared Intermediate?
faqs.a: Three markers: (1) you can build a solution to a problem you have never seen a tutorial for, (2) you can read any blog post in the category without needing to pause for definitions, (3) you can explain the trade-offs of your choice to a non-technical stakeholder in two sentences.

faqs.q: Do I need a GPU?
faqs.a: Not at first. For weeks 1-14, Colab Free or Kaggle Notebooks is enough. For weeks 15-24, rent by the hour on Lambda / Vast / RunPod (~$0.30-$2/hr for a useful GPU). Owning a GPU is a hobby, not a requirement.

tools.intro: This inventory covers the tools a 2026 AI generalist will touch in their first year. It is deliberately conservative — a dozen stable, widely-used tools beats fifty trend-chasing picks. Every item here is free to get started with (open source or a usable free tier). Hours-to-learn estimates are the time until you can solve a novel problem with the tool unaided; reaching mastery takes 3-5x that.

categories.name: Languages + core runtime
categories.blurb: The default working environment for every AI generalist in 2026. Skip exotic languages during the learning phase — the cognitive load of tool-switching compounds.

items.purpose: Primary language for every AI / ML / data task
Package + virtual-env management; uv is 10-100x faster than pip
Fast linter + formatter; replaces flake8 / black / isort
Test runner; the de-facto standard in Python
Interactive notebooks for exploration + prototyping

categories.name: Data + classical ML
categories.blurb: Decade-stable stack; the libraries here have not had a breaking API change in years and will still be around in 2030. Invest deeply.

items.purpose: N-dimensional arrays; the foundation of everything numerical
Tabular data manipulation — the 'Excel of Python'
Classical ML algorithms + pipelines
Static charting; matplotlib for precision, seaborn for speed
Gradient-boosted trees; the winning tabular algorithm
Fast columnar analytics — SQL on CSVs and parquet in one binary

categories.name: Deep learning
categories.blurb: The PyTorch ecosystem owns ~95% of new work in 2026. Add Hugging Face Transformers the moment you need pretrained models — reinventing them is a learning exercise, not a job skill.

items.purpose: Tensor + autograd + neural-net primitives
Pretrained model zoo with a unified API
Streamed, version-pinned datasets — no more copy-paste CSVs
Experiment tracking; run logs without standing up MLflow yourself
Loop boilerplate reduction once you move past hand-written training loops

categories.name: LLM + RAG stack
categories.blurb: Fast-moving category. Prefer APIs with stable contracts (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google AI) while learning; local models (Ollama, llama.cpp) come in when cost or privacy drive the choice.

items.purpose: Production-grade LLM access — GPT-4/5 class models
Claude models with a 1M-token context window
Cost-controlled entry point; 1500 req/day free for learning
Provider-agnostic wrapper — swap models without rewriting prompts
Vector search inside your existing DB — the boring-is-good RAG backbone
Local embedding models — avoid paying per embedding when the corpus is small
Run open models (Llama, Mistral, Qwen) on your laptop for private experiments

categories.name: Backend + deployment
categories.blurb: The shortest path from notebook to internet. Every tool below is free, open source, and runs comfortably on a $5 VPS.

items.purpose: Async Python web framework with automatic OpenAPI + Pydantic validation
ASGI server to run FastAPI in production
Containerisation + local multi-service stacks
Reverse proxy with automatic HTTPS (Caddy) or full control (nginx)
Zero-ops DB + migrations; excellent for < 10k req/min workloads
Async HTTP client; never use `requests` in async code

categories.name: Dev + collaboration
categories.blurb: Non-negotiable ergonomics. Every tool here returns 50-100x its learning time in reduced friction over a career.

items.purpose: Version control + public portfolio substrate
Editor with first-class Python + Jupyter integration
Inline AI assist; useful once you can read the suggestions critically
Primary rubber-duck debugger + code explainer

install_path: Install Python 3.11+ via pyenv (macOS/Linux) or the python.org installer (Windows). Verify with `python --version`.
Install uv. One binary, replaces pip/venv/pyenv-virtualenv workflows: `curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh`.
Initialise a project: `uv init my-first-ai-project && cd my-first-ai-project && uv add numpy pandas scikit-learn jupyterlab`.
Install VS Code + the Python + Jupyter extensions. Open the project folder.
Install Docker Desktop (macOS/Windows) or engine + compose (Linux). Run `docker run hello-world` to confirm.
Create a GitHub account. Configure SSH keys. Push your first repo.
Sign up for Gemini (free tier), OpenAI (pay-as-you-go), and Anthropic. Store keys in a `.env` file never committed to git.
Install Ollama. Pull one small model (`ollama pull llama3.2:3b`) so you can iterate offline.

faqs.q: Is uv strictly better than pip?
faqs.a: For new projects, yes. It is 10-100x faster, resolves lockfiles deterministically, and handles Python installs. The only reason to stay on pip is an existing codebase whose tooling contract would churn.

faqs.q: Which LLM API should I start with?
faqs.a: Gemini's free tier is the fastest 'spend zero dollars to learn' entry. Once you are past toy prompts, run the same workload against OpenAI and Anthropic — knowing each provider's idiosyncrasies is itself a job skill.

faqs.q: Should I use LangChain?
faqs.a: Build one RAG system without LangChain first, then decide. Its abstractions are useful once you understand what they are wrapping; applied cold, they tend to hide the system behavior a junior most needs to see.

faqs.q: Do I need Kubernetes?
faqs.a: Not for the first two years. Docker Compose on a VPS handles every side project and most small-team production workloads. Learn Kubernetes when a real job requires it — the abstractions only make sense once you have felt their problems.

faqs.q: What IDE if not VS Code?
faqs.a: PyCharm Professional remains excellent for Python-heavy, refactor-heavy work. Cursor / Zed / Neovim all have serious user bases. Any of them is fine — the 'best IDE' debate ends once you stop switching.

faqs.q: How much does this stack cost per month?
faqs.a: Running locally: $0 beyond an occasional GPU rental ($1-10/mo while learning). Public-internet deploy on a $5-10 VPS + $5-20 in LLM API costs covers almost all portfolio projects. Total under $40/mo for a committed learner.

faqs.q: When do I add Hugging Face Spaces or Gradio?
faqs.a: Add Gradio the first time you need a quick demo UI — weeks 10-12. Hugging Face Spaces is a zero-ops deploy target for Gradio apps; treat it as a portfolio accelerator, not a replacement for learning Docker.

projects.intro: These projects are ordered by dependency — each earlier one teaches a skill the later ones assume. Pick any ten to ship; the fifteenth on this list, not your first, is what gets the screening recruiter's attention. Every project below has been designed to be deployable on a $5 VPS; salary-positive portfolios are built of live URLs, not notebooks.

projects.title: Exploratory data analysis + prediction on a public dataset
projects.description: Pick a Kaggle dataset (titanic, housing, bike-sharing) you find interesting. Produce a clean notebook covering: data dictionary, missingness audit, target distribution, univariate + bivariate analysis, baseline (mean predictor), three models with cross-validated metrics, feature-importance discussion, and a one-paragraph 'what I'd do next.' This is the universal first-project of the field — and the #1 place juniors reveal an absence of discipline.
rubric: Notebook runs end-to-end from a freshly-cloned repo (reproducibility is not optional)
Baseline predictor comparison present (the #1 omission in beginner work)
Train/val/test split with no leakage, documented explicitly
README with dataset source, installation, and how to re-run
Commit history tells a story — incremental, not 'initial commit' dumps

projects.title: Tabular classifier behind a FastAPI endpoint
projects.description: Take the winner from project 1, serialise it (joblib / pickle), and expose it at `POST /predict`. Add Pydantic input validation, a health endpoint, Dockerfile, and docker-compose.yml. Deploy to a VPS. The project that converts notebook-code skills into production-service skills.
rubric: Pydantic model validates input shape + types + ranges
Dockerfile multi-stage; final image < 500 MB
Live public URL the reviewer can curl against
Structured JSON logging of request + inference time
README records the VPS IP, the deploy command, and how rollback works

projects.title: Image classifier fine-tuned from a pretrained CNN
projects.description: Pick a non-obvious image domain — plant diseases, bird species, sketch categories. Fine-tune resnet50 or efficientnet. Track experiments in W&B. Produce a confusion-matrix analysis and a Grad-CAM interpretability section. Deploy behind FastAPI with a simple Gradio UI.
rubric: Data augmentation strategy documented with before/after validation curves
Grad-CAM or SHAP interpretability section on 5+ misclassified examples
Learning-rate schedule justified (constant vs cosine vs step)
W&B report URL in the README
Gradio demo live; user can upload an image and see the top-3 predictions

projects.title: Text classifier with a Transformer
projects.description: Fine-tune DistilBERT or ModernBERT on a text classification task — toxicity, sentiment on an underserved language, intent classification from chat logs. Benchmark against a TF-IDF + logistic regression baseline; show where the transformer wins and where it does not.
rubric: Baseline comparison present with honest accounting (sometimes the baseline wins)
Tokenisation pitfalls documented (truncation, max length, padding)
Confusion matrix + per-class F1 table
Inference latency measured at batch sizes 1, 8, 32
Failure-mode analysis — 10 real mistakes discussed in prose

projects.title: RAG Q&A system over a personal document corpus
projects.description: Build a retrieval-augmented Q&A system over 50+ documents you actually care about (your university syllabi, your saved articles, a public-domain book corpus). Use pgvector on Postgres. Chunk, embed, retrieve, re-rank, answer. Add a Gradio UI and an evaluation set of 30 questions scored by an LLM judge.
rubric: Chunk strategy explained (size, overlap, structure-aware splits)
30-question eval set with LLM-judge scoring + human spot-check of 5
Hybrid search (BM25 + dense) implemented or justifiably skipped
Streaming response in the UI (no 'Loading...' for 30 seconds)
Prompt injection defense layer — at least input allowlisting

projects.title: Time-series forecasting of a domain you understand
projects.description: Forecast something you can sanity-check yourself — your local weather, a stock price you follow, energy demand for your city. Compare classical (ARIMA, Prophet) vs gradient-boosted (XGBoost with lag features) vs neural (N-BEATS, Temporal Fusion Transformer). Proper time-series cross-validation. Walk-forward evaluation on the last 90 days.
rubric: Time-series train/val/test split (never random) — the leakage checkpoint
Feature engineering beyond raw lags — rolling stats, calendar, holidays
MAPE + MAE reported, not just one metric
Walk-forward evaluation with 90-day horizon
Discussion of failure windows — where each model breaks

projects.title: Recommendation system for a niche taste graph
projects.description: Movies / books / music / recipes — pick one and build a recommender. Implement content-based, collaborative filtering, and a hybrid. Evaluate with precision@k, recall@k, and NDCG. Bonus points: cold-start strategy for new users.
rubric: Three algorithm families implemented + compared honestly
Precision@k, recall@k, NDCG@10 reported
Cold-start strategy written up — even if it's just 'popularity fallback'
Offline A/B of two recommenders with real evaluation set
Live demo where you can click 'I like X' and get updated recommendations

projects.title: LLM function-calling agent with a narrow job
projects.description: Build an agent that does one thing well — books calendar slots, drafts shell commands, summarises your pull requests. Expose 3-5 tools via the provider's function-calling API. Add a safety layer: dry-run before destructive operations, confirmation prompts, output bounds. Resist the urge to make it do ten things.
tech_stack: a domain-specific API (Google Calendar / GitHub / etc.)
rubric: Task scope explicitly narrowed — README has an 'out of scope' section
Dry-run flag for every destructive tool
Tool-call log persisted + inspectable (the #1 debugging surface)
Prompt + tool-schema live in their own files — not inlined in the Python
Failure modes documented — where the agent loops, hallucinates, or gives up

projects.title: Anomaly detection on real production-like data
projects.description: Use a server-log dataset, credit-card fraud set, or an industrial IoT dataset. Implement isolation forest, one-class SVM, and an autoencoder-based anomaly scorer. Address the heavy class imbalance honestly — oversampling, undersampling, class weights, or cost-sensitive learning.
rubric: Class-imbalance strategy chosen + justified
Precision-recall curve present (ROC alone is misleading under imbalance)
Cost-sensitive evaluation (false-negatives weighted realistically)
Comparison of three algorithms with honest trade-off discussion
Live inference endpoint streaming results for a sample of incoming events

projects.title: End-to-end Kaggle-style submission, public writeup
projects.description: Pick an active Kaggle competition or a Hugging Face leaderboard task. Do the full cycle — EDA, baseline, iteration, ensembling, submission. Publish your approach as a Kaggle kernel or blog post. Top-50% finish is a respectable outcome; top-10% is a strong hiring signal. The public writeup is often as valuable as the rank.
tech_stack: whatever the competition rewards — typically PyTorch, XGBoost, or both
rubric: Final rank documented honestly, including what did not work
Reproducibility — a competition-winner's notebook runs in under 30 min
Ensemble strategy explained beyond 'I averaged three models'
Writeup published on Kaggle Discussions or a blog, minimum 1500 words
The README links the rank + writeup + notebook

projects.title: Public-data dashboard with LLM-generated insights
projects.description: Take a public API (OpenWeather, Github Stars, a government open-data portal) and build a live dashboard that refreshes hourly. Surface a one-paragraph LLM-generated narrative of what changed. This project demonstrates three skills at once: ETL, visualisation, and LLM prompt engineering for structured output.
rubric: Scheduled refresh documented — lives past your attention
LLM prompt is cached on inputs so you don't burn API cost per page load
Structured-output validation — the LLM's response gets Pydantic-parsed before display
Failure mode: what shows when the API is down or the LLM errors?
Deployed to a VPS or Railway / Fly, not just running locally

projects.title: Re-implementation of a canonical paper in ≤ 500 lines
projects.description: Pick a paper you care about — 'Attention Is All You Need', 'BERT', 'CLIP', 'Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models', 'Mamba'. Re-implement its core algorithm from scratch in PyTorch in under 500 lines. Train on a toy dataset. Publish a blog post explaining every non-obvious decision. This project cuts your claimed 'I know transformers' from vague to defensible.
rubric: Paper citation + exact section-by-section mapping in the README
Toy-dataset training curves that clearly converge
Code reads like prose — no 4000-line files, no hidden magic
Blog post walks through the math in plain language
Comparison against the Hugging Face reference — yours should match within rounding on a small model

projects.title: Open-source contribution to a library you use
projects.description: Pick a library in your stack — Hugging Face, FastAPI, scikit-learn, LangChain. Find a good-first-issue or reproduce a bug you have hit. Submit a PR. Survive the review. Ship it. One merged PR to a real library outranks five solo side projects for many hiring signals.
rubric: PR is merged (un-merged PRs don't count until they are)
You wrote a test that fails on main and passes on your branch
You respond to reviewer feedback within 48h, professionally
Commit message follows the project's convention
README on your profile links the merged PR

projects.title: Integration project: classic ML + LLM, deployed end-to-end
projects.description: Capstone. Build something that uses classical ML as the reliable backbone and LLM as the flexible interface — example: a resume parser that uses a trained NER model for extraction and an LLM for rewriting, a triage system that routes tickets via a classifier then summarises the top five via LLM, or a pricing assistant that uses XGBoost for the number and an LLM for the explanation. Deployed, documented, evaluated. This is your graduation project.
tech_stack: everything above, pick the right tool per job
rubric: Architecture diagram in the README — one image, clear boxes
Classical + LLM roles justified per-subsystem — no 'let's throw an LLM at it all'
Cost + latency budgets documented per request
End-to-end eval set + metrics
Live URL. A recruiter should be able to use it in under 60 seconds.

projects.title: Teaching artifact — write-up or video tutorial
projects.description: Teach one thing you learned in the last month, clearly, in public. A 1500-word blog post, a 10-minute YouTube video, or a well-illustrated Twitter thread. Teaching is the forcing function that turns 'I ran the tutorial' into 'I understand this.' Your teaching history is a de-facto hiring dossier.
tech_stack: your writing / video tool of choice
rubric: Draft circulated to one reviewer before publish
Opens with the problem, not the answer
Every claim is either demonstrable or cited
You answer the first wave of comments/questions — publish and disappear is a failure state
You publish at least three of these over your learning year

faqs.q: How many of these should I actually ship?
faqs.a: Ten. Twelve is better. Fifteen is overkill unless your job search has stalled. Employers do not read more than five in-depth — so pick your best three to feature and list the rest.

faqs.q: What if my project isn't novel?
faqs.a: That is fine. Novelty is a research signal, not a hiring signal. Execution quality and the ability to explain your choices outrank originality for 99% of jobs.

faqs.q: How do I pick a dataset that won't be a Kaggle cliche?
faqs.a: Use a domain you already care about — local government data, your own fitness metrics, a hobby-specific dataset. Interviewer engagement spikes the moment you can explain the data in plain language because you actually know it.

faqs.q: Is Kaggle rank meaningful to employers?
faqs.a: A top-10% finish on a named competition is a real signal, especially for early-career roles. Kaggle grandmaster is a hard hiring signal. Below top-50%, it's a participation credit — still worth doing, not a résumé line.

faqs.q: How do I handle compute cost?
faqs.a: Weeks 1-14 on Colab Free / Kaggle Notebooks. Week 15+ rent by the hour. Most projects on this list complete in under $20 of GPU time. Set spending alerts on every provider you use.

faqs.q: Should I open-source everything?
faqs.a: Yes, with one exception — scrape-based projects where the source site's ToS is ambiguous. For those, release the code but not the dataset.

faqs.q: How many of these should go on my resume?
faqs.a: Three, featured with a one-line tagline, a tech stack line, and a live-URL link. The rest live on your GitHub profile and portfolio site.

certifications.intro: A 2026 reality check: no certification is a substitute for shipped work, and no hiring manager is fooled by a wall of Coursera badges with no GitHub. That said, the free certifications below carry real employer signal — they demonstrate you know how to finish a long-form, assessed curriculum, and several test applied skills the interview panel will otherwise poke at. The list is deliberately restricted to free-to-audit or fully-free programs; the platform policy (per docs/PRD.md) is that every cert we recommend must be zero-cost to complete.

items.name: Machine Learning Specialization
items.employer_signal: Strong. Andrew Ng's name is the most recognised in the field. 'Took the ML Specialisation' clears the 'does this person know the basics?' question in under five seconds for 90% of screeners.
items.best_for: Absolute beginners who want a gentle, mathematically-rigorous on-ramp to classical ML + early deep learning.

items.name: Deep Learning Specialization
items.employer_signal: Strong for generalists transitioning from classical ML into neural networks. Still holds up well in 2026 for the fundamentals even as the frontier moved past the specific architectures covered.
items.best_for: Learners who finished the ML specialisation and want structured coverage of CNNs, RNNs, and transformer-predecessor architectures.

items.name: Generative AI with Large Language Models
items.employer_signal: Moderate-to-strong. The most up-to-date free LLM curriculum from a named provider in 2026. Signals you understand the full training-to-deployment lifecycle, not just prompting.
items.best_for: Engineers who have built basic LLM integrations and want systematic coverage of fine-tuning, RLHF, and deployment.

items.name: fast.ai — Practical Deep Learning for Coders
items.employer_signal: Strong with the 'shipped something' crowd. Jeremy Howard's course is the most respected top-down deep-learning on-ramp — 'I did the fast.ai course' is widely recognised as code for 'I can actually build things.'
items.best_for: Learners who want to train a production-quality model in week one and backfill the theory later. The opposite pedagogical style to Andrew Ng — pick the one that matches how you learn.

items.name: Google Machine Learning Crash Course
items.employer_signal: Light. Commonly listed on resumes; rarely a differentiator. Useful as a weekend refresher or a first-touch introduction before committing to a longer course.
items.best_for: Existing software engineers wanting a one-weekend 'what is ML' sweep before deciding whether to invest deeper.

items.name: Hugging Face NLP Course
items.employer_signal: Strong for NLP-leaning roles. Hands-on coverage of the Transformers + Datasets + Tokenizers libraries — the de-facto production stack.
items.best_for: Generalists leaning toward LLM-application work; this course's hands-on sections are the shortest path to productive transformer work.

items.name: Kaggle Learn micro-courses
items.employer_signal: Light — the individual courses are weekend-length — but the Kaggle profile + competition record they unlock is a strong, differentiating signal.
items.best_for: Fast, narrow skill acquisition (e.g., 'intro to pandas', 'feature engineering', 'data visualisation'). Stack them with actual competition participation.

items.name: MIT OpenCourseWare — Introduction to Deep Learning (6.S191)
items.employer_signal: Moderate as a line item; strong as context. MIT's brand adds weight, and the weekly lab assignments are non-trivial. More useful as signal that you engage with university-level material than as a standalone credential.
items.best_for: Learners who want the rigorous-but-still-applied MIT pedagogy — more mathematical than fast.ai, more recent than Ng's original course.

items.name: DeepLearning.AI — MLOps Specialization
items.employer_signal: Moderate-to-strong. Covers the unglamorous but highly-paid deployment + monitoring surface most beginners skip. Signals you care about the production side, not just the modelling side.
items.best_for: Generalists who have trained models and want the deployment vocabulary — data drift, serving, monitoring, CI/CD for models.

items.name: Elements of AI
items.employer_signal: Light — explicitly non-technical — but the 'Building AI' follow-on does have practical content and the University of Helsinki name provides institutional credibility.
items.best_for: Learners who want to anchor technical work in a strong, non-hype-y understanding of what AI is and is not. Useful for interviews that probe your ethical / societal framing.

certifications.employer_data: Based on 90-day aggregate data from the AutomateEdge jobs pipeline, certifications appear as explicit requirements in under 3% of AI generalist roles — which is to say, they are almost never a hard gate. They function as tie-breakers. What shows up instead, in 70%+ of postings: a GitHub with three or more non-trivial projects, a bachelor's in a STEM field (or demonstrable equivalent), and conversational familiarity with the specific tools the team uses. The practical implication: treat every cert as worth completing if it teaches you something, worth nothing if it does not.

faqs.q: Which one cert should I do first?
faqs.a: If you have no ML background, Andrew Ng's Machine Learning Specialization. If you are a software engineer who already ships, fast.ai Practical Deep Learning for Coders. Those two cover the widest ground for their audience.

faqs.q: Should I pay for the verified certificate?
faqs.a: Usually not. The audit-free version gives you the material; the paid version adds a sharable PDF. Save the money for GPU time or a book. Exception: if an employer reimburses it, take it.

faqs.q: Are AWS / GCP / Azure ML certifications worth it?
faqs.a: For generalists, no. Those certifications are valuable for cloud-specialist roles, not AI/ML-generalist roles. Revisit them if you end up in an ops-heavy job.

faqs.q: What about the Anthropic / OpenAI prompt-engineering certificates?
faqs.a: Light signal only — they are 2-hour courses. Good first-touch material, not resume-worthy on their own.

faqs.q: Will certifications help me get past the résumé screener?
faqs.a: Marginally. Keyword-matching screeners will notice 'Machine Learning Specialization' — write the full cert name, not just the provider. But a GitHub link with live projects dominates keyword signal every time.

faqs.q: How many certs is 'too many'?
faqs.a: Three featured on your résumé, maximum. Any more reads as cert-collecting, which signals anxious-beginner to experienced reviewers. The rest belong on LinkedIn.

faqs.q: Can I skip certifications entirely?
faqs.a: Yes, if your GitHub is strong and you can speak fluently about the material in interviews. Many senior AI engineers today hold zero formal ML certifications. The work speaks.

salary.intro: The salary ranges below are US market aggregates for AI Generalist and adjacent titles in 2026 Q1. They cover people with 0-10 years of experience and a generalist skill set (no deep specialisation in LLMs, computer vision, or RL). Regional multipliers are applied to the US base and should be interpreted as rough guides — specific-company comp packages vary widely. Hiring velocity + top-company data comes from the AutomateEdge jobs pipeline, refreshed quarterly.

bands_us.notes: Most entry roles at brand-name companies require a BS in a quantitative field or a strong portfolio-plus-bootcamp equivalent.
The largest band by population. Most 'AI/ML engineer' titles in industry are L4-equivalent.
Compensation at this level is dominated by equity. Total-comp spread is wide because equity grants vary 5-10x across companies.
Transition from IC path into technical-leadership scope. Pay at hyperscalers is top-decile at this level.
Rare role. Frontier labs + FAANG only. Comp routinely clears $1M at this tier for top performers.

bands_regions.note: The reference market. Everything compounds here — base, equity, and living cost.
Near-peer to SF for base; equity tends 10-20% lower outside of pure-play AI companies.

bands_regions.region: Other major US metros (Austin, Chicago, Denver, Raleigh)
bands_regions.note: Most roles adjust only moderately; remote policies have compressed the gap to ~5% below SF over the last two years.
Remote-band convergence has largely stabilised at this level for generalist roles. Specialist roles command less of a discount.
CAD comp runs materially below US at junior-mid levels; senior-plus closes to ~75% of US equivalent once equity is included.
Strong discount vs US at all levels. Top Amsterdam / London AI labs are exceptions and pay near US senior rates.
Separate market. Entry: ₹8-18 LPA. Mid: ₹20-45 LPA. Senior at top-tier product companies + GCCs: ₹50-110 LPA. Top-of-market (frontier lab India offices, senior staff): ₹150+ LPA.
Singapore: entry S$60-90k, mid S$100-150k, senior S$160-280k. Other SEA markets run materially below Singapore.

velocity.jobs_last_90d_us: The AutomateEdge pipeline tracked ~2,400 US-based roles tagged AI Generalist / AI Engineer / ML Engineer over the trailing 90 days — up 14% year-over-year. The hiring recovery that began in mid-2024 has stabilised at materially above pre-2023 levels.
velocity.avg_days_to_fill: Median time-to-fill for entry-level generalist roles sits at 38 days; senior roles run 62 days. Recruiter urgency picks up noticeably in Q1 and drops in Q4.
top_hiring_companies: Well-funded AI-native startups (Anthropic, Cohere, Perplexity, Hugging Face, Mistral)

negotiation_tips: Collect three offers before negotiating. Two is a data point; three is leverage.
Ask total-comp, not base-salary. Equity and sign-on are where the real variance lives.
Don't negotiate against yourself. If asked for expectations, deflect to market data or a target range; make them name a number first.
Counter on the entire package — base + equity + sign-on + relocation + start date. Most of those are separate budget lines; moving one doesn't touch the others.
Get everything in writing. A verbal 'we'll make that work' is worth nothing once the offer letter lands.
Know your BATNA — what happens if you walk away? If the answer is 'nothing catastrophic,' you have enormous leverage most juniors never realise they have.
Sign-on bonuses are the cleanest ask — they cost the company once, don't compound, and HR has more room to move on them than on base.

sources.name: US BLS — Computer + Information Research Scientists
LinkedIn Economic Graph — Jobs on the Rise

faqs.q: How fast does pay grow in the first five years?
faqs.a: Entry to mid roughly doubles TC for strong performers (110k → 220k range). Mid to senior adds another 40-60%. Gains plateau after senior unless you move to a frontier lab or hop jobs.

faqs.q: Does remote work still cost you money?
faqs.a: A modest discount persists — roughly 5-10% below co-located pay for equivalent roles. The gap was 20%+ in 2022 and has compressed steadily. Expect continued convergence.

faqs.q: How much of the pay is equity at senior levels?
faqs.a: At public hyperscalers, 40-60% of total comp at L5+ is equity. At private AI-native companies, it's often 60-80%, and the valuation risk is real. Discount private equity by 30-50% when comparing offers.

faqs.q: Do AI generalists get paid less than specialists?
faqs.a: At entry/mid, no — the pay bands overlap almost completely. At senior+, deep specialists (foundation-model training, GPU kernels, applied research) command 20-40% premiums. Generalist scales via breadth and leadership instead.

faqs.q: Is the AI-job boom a bubble?
faqs.a: Hiring velocity peaked in mid-2022, troughed in 2023, and has recovered to well above the pre-boom baseline by 2026. The shape matches a durable structural shift, not a one-time bubble — but role definitions continue to churn. Stay flexible.

faqs.q: Should I care about total comp or base?
faqs.a: Total comp, with a haircut on equity. A job with $160k base + $80k equity is not worth the same as $180k base + $60k equity — equity has volatility, vesting risk, and strike-price risk the base does not.

faqs.q: What's the highest-ROI skill to add if I want a raise?
faqs.a: In 2026, two skills move the negotiation needle most: (1) eval-engineering for LLM products — quantifying AI quality — and (2) production-grade RAG systems. Both skip most junior candidates and both appear in almost every interview loop.

career_path.intro: Career ladders in AI are less standardised than in traditional software engineering — title-to-responsibility mappings vary dramatically across companies. The progression below describes common patterns you should be able to spot at most mid-to-large organisations. Expect hops, lateral moves, and non-linear steps; the stages are orientation, not rails.

stages.title: Associate AI Engineer / Junior ML Engineer / ML Associate
stages.focus: You execute well-scoped tasks under a senior's direction. Typical first-year work: implement a defined ML experiment, improve a data pipeline, build a small internal tool, debug a training-loop issue.
typical_tasks: Implement a data-cleaning pipeline from spec
Train variants of an existing model and report metrics
Build evaluation notebooks for senior review
Write unit tests for an ML library module
On-call for a low-stakes model-serving system
stages.next_step: Cleared Intermediate in all generalist skill categories. Shipped at least two features end-to-end. Your manager no longer spot-checks your diffs line-by-line.

stages.title: AI Engineer / ML Engineer II / Machine Learning Engineer
stages.focus: You own project-sized work start-to-finish. You spec the approach, implement, deploy, and maintain. You begin mentoring juniors. Domain expertise emerges — you specialise organically as the projects you lead require it.
typical_tasks: Own a full ML pipeline for one business surface
Design eval harnesses; pick metrics against product goals
Run A/B tests with production traffic
Mentor one junior through their first deploy
Participate in hiring loops — screens + onsites
skills_gained: a depth area (NLP / ranking / time-series / LLM apps)
stages.next_step: Promotion to senior or horizontal move to a tech-lead role. Your impact is legible to the manager above yours.

stages.title: Senior AI/ML Engineer / Tech Lead / Staff Engineer candidate
stages.focus: You own the architecture of one sub-system. You are the person the team routes novel problems to. You choose between build-vs-buy, influence roadmap, and your opinion on technical direction is weighted heavily.
typical_tasks: Design a ranking / personalisation / LLM-powered system end-to-end
Write technical strategy docs that shape a quarter's work
Establish team-wide patterns (eval harness, CI, deployment)
Drive cross-team initiatives — data-platform migration, LLM-infra integration
Own the interview loop for senior hires
stages.next_step: Staff path requires broader scope; manager path requires explicit people-leadership interest. Some engineers pause here permanently — it's a great local maximum.

stages.title: Staff Engineer / Principal Engineer (small companies) / Engineering Manager (M3)
stages.focus: You influence multiple teams. On the IC path, you work on the hardest problem in the org. On the management path, you are leading 8-15 engineers. The split is usually clean by the time you hit staff — occasional zigzags, but most stay on one path.
typical_tasks: Drive architecture across team boundaries
Set multi-quarter technical strategy
Represent the team to the VP/CTO
Publish externally (arXiv, conference talks, high-signal blog)
If manager: performance management, hiring strategy, budget ownership
stages.next_step: Principal / Director / VP / Co-founder. Depth of IC work or breadth of org work — rarely both after this point.

stages.title: Principal Engineer / Distinguished Engineer / Director of AI / VP / Founder
stages.focus: Your output is decisions and people, not code. On the IC path, you set the technical direction of an entire AI org — 50-500 people execute against your north stars. On the management path, you run a multi-hundred-person organisation. Founder paths diverge into their own world.
typical_tasks: Define 3-5 year technical bets
Set compensation bands and hiring philosophy
Represent the company externally — press, analysts, investors
Sponsor high-risk research / product initiatives
Recruit other senior leaders
stages.next_step: Retirement, founding, advisory-only roles, or a different kind of second act altogether.

transitions: Research → Applied: Many PhDs start in research labs and move to applied roles after 2-3 years. The comp is usually higher; the impact is more direct; the publication volume drops.
Applied → Research: Harder direction. Requires publications or co-authorship to be competitive. Most who make the move are at senior+ levels and have earned the lab's attention via side work.
Engineering → Product Management: A common L5-L6 pivot, especially into 'Technical PM for AI' roles. The salary penalty is small (~5-10%); the skill change is real.
Engineering → Founder: The data says most successful AI founders are ex-senior engineers, not fresh grads. Year 5-10 is the modal founding-year.
Specialist → Generalist: Rare but increasing as LLMs collapse formerly-separate disciplines. A speech-recognition specialist today can feasibly transition to general LLM applications in 12-18 months.
Industry → Academia: Uncommon post-2015. The research compute + data asymmetry between industry and academia is too large to easily reverse.

faqs.q: Do I need a PhD to advance to senior?
faqs.a: No. The mix at L5 is roughly 40% PhD, 35% MS, 25% BS at most US tech companies in 2026. At frontier labs the PhD share is higher; at applied-AI startups it's lower.

faqs.q: How fast can I realistically progress?
faqs.a: With strong performance: L3 → L4 in 2-3 years, L4 → L5 in 2-3 more. Faster ladders exist at high-growth startups (you absorb scope growth at speed) but come with more volatility.

faqs.q: Is the IC path capped?
faqs.a: Functionally no, but the roles above L6 are rare — maybe 1% of the population. Principal+ roles require a combination of technical depth and organisational influence most people don't cultivate.

faqs.q: How do I choose IC vs management?
faqs.a: Trial the role before committing. Lead a project as an IC tech-lead first. If you find the people-management parts energising, move to manager. If you find them draining, stay IC. Both paths pay well; miserable managers are common enough to avoid becoming one.

faqs.q: What derails careers most often?
faqs.a: Staying too long at L3/L4 without owning something end-to-end (no scope story), under-communicating impact (the work exists but no one above your manager knows), and burnout from chasing titles faster than you grew the underlying skills.

faqs.q: How common are lateral moves?
faqs.a: Very. 60%+ of the L5-L6 engineers I've interviewed changed companies at least once to get there, usually for a level-bump and a significant comp correction. Staying put past 3 years at one company without a promotion is a yellow flag.

faqs.q: Does the manager path pay more?
faqs.a: At L5-L6, TC is roughly equal. At L7+, the IC path starts to outpace management on a per-head basis (fewer principals than directors) but the top of management is uncapped for the same reason CEO pay is uncapped.
